firstAid.subtitle: ACT FAST
firstAid.title: Emergency First Aid
firstAid.desc: If someone is having a seizure, stay calm. Follow these standardized steps based on guidelines from the CDC and the Epilepsy Foundation.
firstAid.step1Desc: Note the exact time the seizure starts. If it lasts longer than 5 minutes, call an ambulance immediately.
firstAid.step2Desc: Move hard or sharp objects out of the way. Place something soft, like a folded jacket, under their head.
firstAid.step3Desc: Once the jerking stops, gently roll the person onto their side. This helps keep their airway clear.
doList: Stay with the person until the seizure ends and they are fully awake.
Loosen anything around their neck (like a tie or collar).
Reassure them gently once they wake up; they may be confused.
Check if they have a medical ID bracelet.
dontList: Do not hold the person down or try to stop their movements.
Do not put anything in their mouth. It can injure their teeth or jaw.
Do not give them water, food, or pills until fully alert.
Do not perform CPR unless they stop breathing after the seizure stops.
emergencyList: The seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes.
The person has difficulty breathing after the seizure.
They have another seizure immediately after the first.
They get injured during the seizure.
The person is pregnant, sick, or has diabetes.
It is the person's first ever seizure.

aboutEpilepsy.subtitle: LEARN
aboutEpilepsy.title: What is Epilepsy?
aboutEpilepsy.desc: Epilepsy is more than just symptoms; it is a neurological condition that involves recurrent, unprovoked seizures. Understanding the classification is the first step toward effective management.
aboutEpilepsy.classSubtitle: Select a type to view detailed clinical observation patterns and safety notes.

what.title: What is Epilepsy?
what.content: Epilepsy is a chronic noncommunicable disease of the brain that affects around 50 million people worldwide. It is characterized by recurrent seizures, which are brief episodes of involuntary movement that may involve a part of the body (partial) or the entire body (generalized), and are sometimes accompanied by loss of consciousness and control of bowel or bladder function.

causes.title: Causes of Epilepsy
causes.content: Epilepsy is not contagious. Although many underlying disease mechanisms can lead to epilepsy, the cause of the disease is still unknown in about 50% of cases globally. The causes of epilepsy are divided into the following categories: structural, genetic, infectious, metabolic, immune and unknown. Examples include brain damage from prenatal or perinatal causes, congenital abnormalities, stroke, head trauma, and infections such as meningitis or encephalitis.

diagnosis.title: Diagnosis & Treatment
diagnosis.content:
A doctor will review your symptoms and medical history. Tests like an electroencephalogram (EEG) or neurological scans (MRI, CT) are commonly used to diagnose the specific type of epilepsy. 

The disease can be successfully treated in up to 70% of cases with anti-seizure medications (AEDs). For those who do not respond to medication, surgery, neurostimulation, or dietary changes (like the ketogenic diet) might be considered.

gtc.name: Generalized Tonic-Clonic
gtc.title: Tonic-Clonic
gtc.description: A major generalized seizure characterized by sudden loss of consciousness, body stiffening (tonic phase), followed by rhythmic jerking (clonic phase).
gtc.note: Ensure the environment is clear of sharp objects. Do not place anything in the mouth.

focalAware.name: Focal Aware Seizure
focalAware.title: Focal Aware
focalAware.description: Starts in one area of the brain. The person remains fully aware and can usually describe the event in detail after it finishes.
focalAware.note: Observe carefully to see if it progresses. Documentation of precise feelings is vital for diagnosis.

absence.name: Absence Seizure
absence.title: Absence
absence.description: A brief lapse in consciousness that looks like staring into space. Commonly seen in children and lasts only a few seconds.
absence.note: Easily missed in school settings. Frequent "daydreaming" should be medically investigated.

focalImpaired.name: Focal Impaired Awareness
focalImpaired.title: Focal Impaired
focalImpaired.description: A focal seizure where awareness is altered or lost. The person may appear conscious but is not fully responsive.
focalImpaired.note: Guide the individual away from danger gently; do not restrain them forcefully.

atonic.name: Atonic Seizure (Drop Attacks)
atonic.title: Atonic
atonic.description: Sudden loss of muscle tone causing the person to collapse or fall forward. Also known as "drop attacks".
atonic.note: Risk of head injury is high. Protective headgear is often recommended for frequent events.

myoclonic.name: Myoclonic Seizure
myoclonic.title: Myoclonic
myoclonic.description: Brief, shock-like jerks of a muscle or group of muscles. Usually occurring on both sides of the body.
myoclonic.note: Often occurs shortly after waking. Can interfere with holding objects or eating.

mythsFacts.subtitle: CLARITY
mythsFacts.title: Myths & Facts
mythsFacts.desc: There is a lot of misinformation surrounding epilepsy. We are here to clear the air with evidence-based facts to reduce stigma and promote understanding.

items.myth: You should put a spoon in the mouth of someone having a seizure.
items.fact: NEVER put anything in the mouth. It can break teeth or block the airway. It is an old myth that people can swallow their tongue; they cannot.
Epilepsy is a neurological condition, not an infection. You cannot 'catch' epilepsy from someone else.

items.myth: People with epilepsy are mentally ill.
items.fact: Epilepsy is a physical condition characterized by electrical disturbances in the brain. It is not a mental illness or a sign of low intelligence.
While it is common in children, epilepsy can develop at any age, especially in seniors due to stroke or other health conditions.

items.myth: You should hold down a person having a seizure.
items.fact: Never hold a person down to try and stop their movements. This can result in bone fractures or muscle tearing. Just clear the area of hard objects.

items.myth: During a seizure, a person can swallow their tongue.
items.fact: It is physically impossible to swallow your tongue. However, a person's airway can become blocked, which is why rolling them on their side is important.

items.myth: A seizure is always a medical emergency.
items.fact: Actually, most seizures last a short time and stop on their own. You only need to call 123 if it lasts longer than 5 minutes, or under specific criteria.

specialWarnings.subtitle: Vulnerable Populations
specialWarnings.title: Special Population Warnings
specialWarnings.desc: Pharmacokinetics can vary significantly across different age groups. Extra clinical vigilance is required for children and the elderly when administering antiepileptic therapy.

items.title: Dosage Sensitivity
items.content: Children metabolize drugs differently than adults. They often require higher doses per kilogram of body weight, but they are also more susceptible to cognitive side effects.

items.title: Behavioral Monitoring
items.content: Certain drugs (like Levetiracetam or Phenobarbital) can cause paradoxical hyperactivity, aggression, or learning difficulties in children. Parents and teachers must monitor school performance closely.

items.title: Growth Considerations
items.content: Dosages need frequent readjustment as the child grows and gains weight to maintain therapeutic blood levels.

items.title: Slower Metabolism
items.content: Decreased renal and hepatic clearance means drugs stay in the system longer. Elderly patients typically require lower starting doses (“start low, go slow”).

items.title: High Interaction Risk
items.content: Older adults often take multiple medications for other conditions (hypertension, diabetes), drastically increasing the risk of adverse drug-drug interactions with AEDs.

items.title: Fall & Bone Risks
items.content: Enzyme-inducing AEDs (like Phenytoin or Carbamazepine) can reduce bone density. Dizziness and sedation from meds also greatly increase the risk of dangerous falls.

checker.subtitle: Live Validation
checker.title: Medication Safety Center
checker.desc: Live dynamic validation checking your prescriptions against epilepsy therapies and food interactions via NIH RxNav.
checker.buildDesc: Add drugs below. We automatically cross-reference these against baseline epilepsy treatments.
checker.awaitingDesc: Enter your prescriptions and click generate to retrieve safety protocols across multiple health databases.
checker.noInteractions: No major interactions found in the clinical proxy database between your inputs and baseline epilepsy medications. Always consult your provider.
checker.noFoodWarnings: No major food or lifestyle restrictions identified in OpenFDA or local clinical protocols for these medications.
checker.disclaimerDesc: This application relies on NIH RxNav and OpenFDA APIs strictly for educational purposes. Drug interactions are complex and dependent on your specific health history. Do not alter your medication regiment without consulting your doctor or pharmacist.
checker.archiveDesc: Log in to save drugs across sessions.
checker.noSavedMeds: No medications saved to your profile yet.

errors.notFound: "{{name}}" not found in RxNav database. Check spelling.
errors.network: Network error connecting to drug database.
errors.addOne: Please add at least 1 medication to check.
errors.apiDown: Failed to generate complete interaction report. The API service may be down.

emergency.label: Emergency Protocol (Error Handling):
emergency.title: Emergency Protocol: What to do if you already took the drug?
steps: DO NOT stop taking any epilepsy medication abruptly without medical consultation; sudden withdrawal can trigger life-threatening status epilepticus.
Contact your treating physician or pharmacist immediately to report the interaction and discuss safe alternatives.
Monitor for toxicity symptoms: double vision, severe dizziness, persistent vomiting, or sudden rash.
In case of a 'Critical' (Severe) interaction, go to the nearest emergency department and bring your medication packages.

dashboard.secureDesc: Please log in to view your seizure history and medication list.

auth.signupDesc: Your safe space for epilepsy care

about.subtitle: The Clinical Team
about.title: Meet the Pharmacists Behind SeizureSafe
about.desc: We are a dedicated group of pharmacy students committed to improving epilepsy care through technological intervention.
about.project: Faculty of Pharmacy Medical Graduation Project 2026
about.missionText1: We noticed that navigating the complexities of epilepsy treatment—from recognizing seizure patterns to tracking potential drug interactions—can be overwhelming for patients without constant professional supervision.
about.missionText2: That is why we created SeizureSafe. An accessible tool that empowers patients with immediate, reliable pharmacological checks and symptom logging, functioning as your personal clinical pharmacist counterpart.

values.title: Evidence-Based
values.desc: All our diagnostic tools and interaction verifications are grounded in active pharmacological research and medical databases.

values.title: Transparent
values.desc: We provide clear, un-biased interpretations of medical interactions without hidden agendas.

values.title: Patient Centric
values.desc: The interface respects that tracking seizures and medications can be stressful, focusing on a calm and reassuring design.

values.title: Safety First
values.desc: The primary outcome of every feature in SeizureSafe is to prevent dangerous contraindications.

about.ctaText: Access our expert system designed to help identify the patterns of epileptic episodes to prepare comprehensive reports for your doctor.

home.heroTitle: Your Loved One’s Safety Is Our Priority
home.heroDescription: We bridge the gap between medical complexity and compassionate care. Identify seizure patterns with precision and check drug interactions with pharmacist-backed tools designed for the people you care for most.
home.understandingDescription: Epilepsy is a chronic noncommunicable disease of the brain that affects people of all ages. Knowing the specific type of seizure is vital for providing the correct pharmaceutical treatment.
home.servicesSubtitle: Everything you need for safe pharmacological management.
home.eduDesc: Comprehensive guide on what to do during a seizure, common myths, and facts to empower caregivers.
home.checkerDesc: High-accuracy safety tool utilizing global RxNav standard data to cross-verify prescriptions.
home.archivesDesc: Securely store their medication history and ongoing medical assessments in your personal dashboard.
home.ctaDesc: Join SeizureSafe today to build a secure, pharmacist-approved archive for your loved ones.

footer.copyright: © 2026 SeizureSafe. MTI University Pharmacy Graduation Project.
footer.disclaimer: Medical Disclaimer: All recommendations are for medical knowledge only and not a substitute for professional medical advice. For dosages, consult your physician.

desc.ccbCarba: Severe Toxicity Risk: Blood pressure drugs raise Tegretol levels to toxic levels within 48 hours.
desc.stJohnsAED: St. John's Wort triggers liver enzymes, destroying the efficacy of AEDs and birth control pills.
desc.theoAED: Epilepsy drugs accelerate the breakdown of asthma drugs (Theophylline) before they can treat the lungs.
desc.metroPheny: Flagyl prevents the liver from clearing Phenytoin (Epanutin), leading to toxic accumulation.
desc.inhAED: Very strong enzyme inhibitor that raises epilepsy drug levels to dangerous heights.
desc.psyCarba: Bone Marrow Suppression: Combination completely destroys white blood cells.
desc.diuAED: Risk of acute Hyponatremia (low sodium) and brain salt imbalance.
desc.reducedEfficacy: Significantly reduces the efficacy of the other drug.
desc.increasedLevels: Increases levels of the other drug to potentially toxic heights.
desc.aspirinValproate: Aspirin increases Valproate levels, risking hepatotoxicity.
desc.oldGenGeneric: This is an older generation AED heavily metabolized in the liver.
desc.newGenGeneric: Newer generation AED, generally safe with most medications.
desc.tramadolAED: Strictly contraindicated for epilepsy patients: Tramadol directly lowers the seizure threshold and causes sudden Grand Mal seizures.
desc.metoclopramideAED: Primperan (Metoclopramide) directly provokes seizures and causes involuntary muscle spasms.
desc.ssriAED: SSRI antidepressants raise AED levels to toxic concentrations.
desc.azoleAED: Azole antifungals acutely raise Phenytoin/Carbamazepine to neurotoxic levels.
desc.statinAED: AEDs destroy statins before they work, secretly raising cholesterol and causing sudden cardiac events.
desc.immunoAED: AEDs destroy immunosuppressants, risking transplant rejection or severe autoimmune relapse.
desc.contraAED: AEDs make hormonal birth control fail, causing unwanted and potentially malformed pregnancy.
desc.thyroxineAED: AEDs accelerate breakdown of thyroid hormone (Levothyroxine), causing hypothyroidism.
desc.allopurinolAED: Gout drug inhibits AED clearance, gradually raising AED levels.
desc.carbapenemValp: Carbapenem antibiotics wipe Valproate from blood within 24 hours, triggering continuous seizures.
desc.valpLamot: Valproate doubles Lamotrigine levels, causing potentially lethal Stevens-Johnson Syndrome.

mechanism.ccbCarba: These BP drugs completely disable liver enzymes (CYP3A4), raising Tegretol blood levels to toxic levels.
mechanism.stJohnsAED: This herb is a fierce inducer of liver enzymes, effectively cleaning AEDs out of the blood.
mechanism.theoAED: Classic AEDs break down asthma drugs too rapidly for them to exert a therapeutic effect.
mechanism.metroPheny: Flagyl inhibits the metabolism of Phenytoin, causing it to accumulate in the blood.
mechanism.inhAED: INH is a potent enzyme inhibitor; interaction depends on the patient's genetics (Slow Acetylators).
mechanism.psyCarba: Both Carbamazepine and Clozapine cause bone marrow suppression as a lethal side effect.
mechanism.diuAED: These AEDs cause low sodium, and adding diuretics leads to dehydration and acute brain salt depletion.
mechanism.oldGenGeneric: Significantly impacts liver enzymes (CYP450) either by inhibition or induction.
mechanism.newGenGeneric: Excreted mostly via kidneys and does not interfere with standard liver metabolic pathways.
mechanism.tramadolAED: Tramadol inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake and binds opioid receptors in the brain, directly reducing seizure threshold regardless of AED therapy.
mechanism.metoclopramideAED: Blocks dopamine receptors in the CNS, lowering the seizure threshold and causing extrapyramidal symptoms.
mechanism.ssriAED: Inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP2C19 enzymes in the liver, preventing breakdown of Phenytoin and Carbamazepine, raising their levels.
mechanism.azoleAED: Potently inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, rapidly preventing AED breakdown and raising levels.
mechanism.statinAED: Old-gen AEDs are strong CYP3A4 inducers, accelerating statin breakdown before reaching therapeutic levels.
mechanism.immunoAED: AEDs (Enzyme Inducers) stimulate CYP3A4 liver enzymes, destroying immunosuppressants before they work.
mechanism.contraAED: AEDs stimulate CYP3A4, accelerating breakdown of estrogen and progesterone in hormonal contraceptives.
mechanism.thyroxineAED: AEDs accelerate conversion of active T4 to T3 and its degradation, requiring higher Levothyroxine doses.
mechanism.allopurinolAED: Allopurinol inhibits Xanthine Oxidase, affecting the metabolic pathways of some AEDs like Carbamazepine.
mechanism.carbapenemValp: Activates UGT enzyme to rapidly convert Valproate to inactive metabolites, crashing drug levels to zero.
mechanism.valpLamot: Valproate inhibits UGT responsible for Lamotrigine breakdown, doubling Lamotrigine blood levels.

ccbCarba.female: Females/Pregnancy: May cause neurological toxicity in mothers and oxygen deprivation in fetuses.
ccbCarba.senior: Seniors: Severe Bradycardia (low heart rate), coma, and lethal falls.
ccbCarba.child: Children: Rapid heart and respiratory failure in case of accidental ingestion.

stJohnsAED.female: Females: Double risk; seizures due to AED failure and unplanned pregnancy due to birth control failure.
stJohnsAED.male: Males: Reduced efficacy of AEDs and recurrence of seizures.
stJohnsAED.pregnant: Pregnancy: Causes uterine contractions and loss of seizure control.

theoAED.child: Children: Acute asthma attacks and nocturnal suffocation due to rapid drug breakdown.
theoAED.senior: Seniors: COPD patients will suffer respiratory failure as the lung medication fails.
theoAED.pregnant: Pregnancy: Oxygen deprivation due to asthma attacks directly threatens the fetus.

metroPheny.child: Children: Neurological toxicity (staggering, unable to walk) due to Phenytoin accumulation.
metroPheny.pregnant: Pregnancy: Increased Phenytoin levels raise the risk of fetal malformations.

inhAED.genetic: Genetics: Slow Acetylators are prone to lethal toxicity within days.
inhAED.child: Children: Requires medical isolation and daily monitoring of drug levels in the hospital.

psyCarba.general: Everyone: Agranulocytosis syndrome; complete loss of immunity, minor microbes become lethal.
psyCarba.senior: Seniors: Rapid death from infection or pneumonia due to lack of immunity.

diuAED.senior: Seniors: Severe low sodium causes brain swelling, coma, and continuous seizures.
diuAED.female: Females: More prone to electrolyte imbalance due to cyclic hormonal changes.

oldGenGeneric.general: Expect a 90% interaction rate with any other hepatic drug.

newGenGeneric.senior: Seniors: Impacted only by kidney function. Monitor renal clearance.

tramadolAED.general: Tramadol is absolutely contraindicated for epilepsy patients. It directly lowers the seizure threshold in the brain and causes sudden Grand Mal seizures.
tramadolAED.senior: Seniors: Causes immediate coma and severe cardiovascular collapse.
tramadolAED.pregnant: Pregnant: Crosses placenta, causes in-utero seizures and neonatal respiratory depression.

metoclopramideAED.child: Children: Causes involuntary muscle spasms (Extrapyramidal Symptoms) mistaken for seizures, and triggers real epileptic seizures.
metoclopramideAED.pregnant: Pregnant: Used for nausea but can destabilize seizure control in epilepsy patients.

ssriAED.female: Females: Common for postpartum depression/PMS. Raises AED levels causing neurotoxicity (ataxia, double vision, mouth sores).

azoleAED.female: Females: Fluconazole is widely used for vaginal infections. A single capsule can raise Phenytoin to toxic neurological levels. Use topical alternatives.
azoleAED.senior: Seniors: Risk of drug accumulation due to slower hepatic clearance.

statinAED.senior: Seniors/Males: AED destroys the statin, secretly raising cholesterol and causing sudden cardiac events.
statinAED.general: Switch to Rosuvastatin (Crestor) — less affected by liver enzyme inducers.

immunoAED.child: Children/Adults (organ transplant): AED destroys immunosuppressant, risking transplant rejection.
immunoAED.general: Autoimmune disease patients (Lupus, Rheumatoid): Risk of severe relapse.

contraAED.female: Females: Birth control failure = malformed pregnancy. Use higher hormone doses or non-hormonal contraception.

thyroxineAED.female: Females (thyroid patients): AED destroys Eltroxin, causing severe hypothyroidism (obesity, hair loss, depression). Regularly increase Eltroxin dose.

allopurinolAED.male: Males (most affected by gout): Inhibits AED clearance, requires dose reduction of the AED.
allopurinolAED.senior: Seniors: Risk of hepatotoxicity if AED dose is not adjusted.

carbapenemValp.child: Children: The antibiotic wipes Valproate from blood within 24 hours causing continuous lethal seizures.
carbapenemValp.general: Adults: Risk of acute Status Epilepticus. Alternative: Aztreonam does not interact with Valproate.

valpLamot.child: Children: Risk of Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (skin blistering). Requires micro-dosing protocol with very slow dose escalation over weeks.
valpLamot.female: Females: Combination requires close medical supervision and weekly drug level monitoring.

emergency.ccbCarba: Go to the ER immediately for an ECG and Tegretol level measurement. Switch to Amlodipine.
emergency.stJohnsAED: Stop the supplement immediately. In case of seizures, go to the hospital for an IV rescue dose.
emergency.theoAED: Use topical inhalers instead of tablets as they do not interact with AEDs via the liver.
emergency.metroPheny: Use antibacterials that do not interact with the liver, or reduce the dose with close monitoring.
emergency.inhAED: Respiratory and neurological emergency; immediate cessation and IV fluid support.
emergency.psyCarba: Contraindicated globally. Go to ICU immediately if fever or severe sore throat occurs.
emergency.diuAED: Go to the ER for blood sodium measurement. Treated with slow infusion of hypertonic saline.
emergency.oldGenGeneric: If unusual symptoms or skin rash occur, see your doctor immediately for liver function tests.
emergency.tramadolAED: Go to the ER immediately if you took Tramadol. There is no safe dose for an epilepsy patient. Inform the doctor of all your AEDs.
emergency.metoclopramideAED: Stop immediately. If muscle spasms or seizures occur, call an ambulance. Safe alternative: Ondansetron (Zofran) for nausea.
emergency.ssriAED: If toxicity symptoms appear (ataxia, double vision), go to ER. Monitor AED levels weekly when starting an SSRI.
emergency.azoleAED: Use topical alternatives (creams/pessaries) instead of oral pills. If you took a Fluconazole tablet, watch for neurotoxicity symptoms and go to ER if they appear.
emergency.statinAED: Do not stop cholesterol medication abruptly. Ask your doctor to switch to Rosuvastatin and monitor cholesterol every 3 months.
emergency.immunoAED: Medical emergency! If you are a transplant patient taking an AED, notify your doctor immediately to increase your immunosuppressant dose.
emergency.contraAED: Consult your doctor immediately. Alternatives: Higher-dose hormonal pills, IUD, or barrier methods.
emergency.thyroxineAED: Monitor hypothyroid symptoms (weight gain, fatigue, depression). Ask your doctor to raise Eltroxin dose and check TSH every 6 months.
emergency.allopurinolAED: Consult your doctor to reduce AED dose or find a gout drug alternative that does not interact with liver enzymes.
emergency.carbapenemValp: Critical emergency! If Meropenem is necessary, inform the doctor to temporarily replace Valproate with another AED or give an additional IV dose.
emergency.valpLamot: If a rash or redness appears, go to the ER immediately. SJS develops within 24-48 hours. Never ignore any rash.

comment.ccbCarba: Critical risk! BP drugs raise Tegretol to toxic levels. Go to ER immediately.
comment.stJohnsAED: High risk! Herb destroys AED and birth control efficacy. Stop immediately.
comment.theoAED: High risk! AED disables asthma drug. Use topical inhalers.
comment.metroPheny: High risk! Flagyl causes toxic Phenytoin accumulation. Consult doctor for alternative.
comment.inhAED: Critical risk! TB drugs raise AED levels to lethal heights.
comment.psyCarba: Lethal combination! Destroys immune cells in bone marrow. Strictly contraindicated.
comment.diuAED: High risk! Causes acute low sodium and brain swelling. Monitor electrolytes.
comment.contraceptives: Risk of unplanned pregnancy as drug reduces contraceptive efficacy.
comment.bloodThinners: Risk of blood clots as drug reduces blood thinner efficacy.
comment.lamotrigineSJS: Risk of lethal skin rash (SJS). Seek ER immediately if rash appears.
comment.oldGenGeneric: Expect 90% interaction rate with other hepatic drugs. Monitoring required.
comment.newGenGeneric: This drug is 95% safe and unaffected by liver function.
comment.tramadolAED: Strictly contraindicated! Tramadol directly causes seizures. No safe dose for epilepsy patients.
comment.metoclopramideAED: High risk! Primperan triggers seizures. Safe alternative: Ondansetron.
comment.ssriAED: High risk! Raises AED levels to toxic concentration. Monitor levels weekly.
comment.azoleAED: Critical risk! One pill can cause acute neurotoxicity. Use topical alternatives.
comment.statinAED: High risk! AED destroys the statin. Switch to Rosuvastatin.
comment.immunoAED: Critical risk! May cause transplant rejection. Notify your doctor immediately.
comment.contraAED: Critical risk for females! Birth control failure and risk of malformed pregnancy.
comment.thyroxineAED: High risk! Hidden hypothyroidism. Regularly increase Eltroxin dose.
comment.allopurinolAED: Moderate risk. Monitor AED levels and reduce dose if necessary.
comment.carbapenemValp: Critical risk! Antibiotic erases AED from blood. Replace AED temporarily.
comment.valpLamot: Critical risk! Any rash = go to ER immediately.
